methodology.injection_tool: stress-ng for resource faults (CPU, MEM, DISK); tc (traffic control) for network faults (DELAY, LOSS, SOCKET)
methodology.monitoring_tools: Prometheus + cAdvisor for metrics; Fluent Bit / Loki for logs; Jaeger for traces
methodology.sampling_rate: 1-second intervals for metrics, 15-second aggregation for logs and traces
methodology.load_profile: 100-200 concurrent users with custom load generators
methodology.infrastructure: AWS Kubernetes clusters running microservice benchmarks (Online Boutique, Sock Shop, Train Ticket)
methodology.data_format: Each fault case is a directory: {system}/{service}_{fault_type}/{repetition}/ containing metrics.json (or data.csv), inject_time.txt, and optionally logs.csv and traces.csv
methodology.metric_naming: Columns follow {service}_{metric_suffix} pattern, e.g., carts_cpu, carts_mem, frontend_latency

memory_leak.description: Memory exhaustion fault injected via stress-ng into a target microservice container. Simulates progressive memory consumption that overwhelms the service's available memory, causing container resource pressure, potential OOM kills, and cascading latency increases in dependent services.
memory_leak.injection_method: stress-ng memory stress applied to a specific service's container/pod in the Kubernetes cluster, causing the container's resident set size (RSS) to grow until resource limits are hit.
metrics_affected: {service}_mem - container memory usage in bytes (primary indicator, divided by 1e6 for MB in preprocessing)
{service}_cpu - secondary CPU increase due to memory management overhead, garbage collection pressure
{service}_latency - increased response latency as memory pressure causes slowdowns
{downstream_service}_latency - cascading latency in services that depend on the affected service
container_memory_working_set_bytes - Kubernetes/cAdvisor metric showing working set growth
container_memory_rss - resident set size showing actual physical memory consumption
typical_logs: OOMKilled events in Kubernetes pod logs when memory limit is exceeded
Container restart events following OOM kills
Application-level error logs from failed memory allocations
Increased error rate logs from downstream services due to timeouts
memory_leak.timeline_pattern: Gradual onset: memory usage ({service}_mem) begins rising at inject_time, climbs steadily over seconds to minutes. Latency degradation follows with a delay as memory pressure builds. If memory limit is reached, OOM kill triggers a sharp discontinuity (service restart). The z-score anomaly detection in RCAEval identifies the affected service's _mem metric as having the maximum deviation from the normal (pre-injection) baseline distribution.
distinguishing_features: Primary indicator is the _mem metric suffix - RCAEval maps 'mem' fault type to _mem metric columns
Monotonically increasing memory consumption pattern (ramp, not spike)
Memory growth persists throughout the anomaly window (not transient)
CPU may show secondary increase but _mem has the dominant z-score
Latency impact is gradual and progressive, not sudden
Unlike CPU faults, the memory metric shows sustained directional change rather than oscillation
convert_mem_mb() preprocessing divides _mem columns by 1e6 to normalize to megabytes

cpu_throttling.description: CPU exhaustion fault injected via stress-ng into a target microservice container. Simulates CPU hogging that consumes all available CPU cycles for the container, causing request processing delays, increased queue depths, and cascading failures across the service dependency graph.
cpu_throttling.injection_method: stress-ng CPU stress applied to a specific service's container/pod in the Kubernetes cluster, consuming CPU cycles and triggering Kubernetes CPU throttling when the container exceeds its CPU limit.
metrics_affected: {service}_cpu - container CPU usage rate (primary indicator, highest z-score deviation)
{service}_latency - increased response latency due to CPU starvation for request processing
container_cpu_usage_seconds_total - cAdvisor metric showing CPU consumption rate
{service}_mem - possible secondary memory increase from request queuing
typical_logs: Request timeout errors in upstream services calling the CPU-stressed service
Connection timeout and circuit breaker activation logs
Increased error rate logs from the affected service and its dependents
cpu_throttling.timeline_pattern: Abrupt onset: CPU usage ({service}_cpu) spikes sharply at inject_time and remains elevated (plateau pattern). Latency impact is near-immediate as pending requests queue up. Unlike memory faults, the transition from normal to anomalous is rapid. The z-score anomaly detection identifies the affected service's _cpu metric as having the maximum deviation from the normal baseline.
distinguishing_features: Primary indicator is the _cpu metric suffix - RCAEval maps 'cpu' fault type to _cpu metric columns
Sharp step-function increase in CPU usage at injection time (not gradual like memory)
CPU usage hits a ceiling determined by stress-ng intensity and container CPU limits
Latency impact is immediate (within seconds of injection), unlike memory's gradual degradation
The CPU metric shows a plateau pattern rather than the monotonic ramp of memory faults
Memory may show minor secondary effects, but _cpu has the dominant z-score
Affects request processing capacity immediately, causing rapid latency spikes

evaluation_methodology.anomaly_detection: RCAEval splits data into normal (pre-inject_time) and anomalous (post-inject_time) windows. For each metric column, it computes z-scores comparing the anomalous window against the normal baseline distribution (mean, std from StandardScaler). The metric with the maximum absolute z-score is identified as the root cause indicator.
evaluation_methodology.root_cause_labeling: Each fault case directory name encodes the answer: {service}_{fault_type}, e.g., 'carts_cpu' means the carts service with CPU fault. The evaluation creates Node(entity=service, metric=fault_type) as the ground truth answer.
evaluation_methodology.accuracy_metric: AC@k measures whether the true root cause service appears in the top-k ranked results

key_differences_mem_vs_cpu.onset_pattern: CPU faults show abrupt step-change; memory faults show gradual ramp-up
key_differences_mem_vs_cpu.primary_metric: CPU -> _cpu suffix columns; Memory -> _mem suffix columns
key_differences_mem_vs_cpu.latency_impact_delay: CPU causes near-immediate latency; memory causes delayed/progressive latency
key_differences_mem_vs_cpu.signal_shape: CPU shows plateau/step-function; memory shows monotonic increase
key_differences_mem_vs_cpu.recovery: CPU recovers immediately when stress stops; memory may require restart/GC
key_differences_mem_vs_cpu.resource_limit_behavior: CPU triggers CFS throttling; memory triggers OOM kills
